name: Trip.com eSIM
verdict: The travel-agency option — official Chinese carriers, refundable, priced by the day from $2.94
exitCountry: Unverified by field test (sold on domestic carriers but routed internationally — needs IP check on the ground)

throttlePolicy.dailyCap: Not published (per-day tiers sold as 0.5GB/1GB/2GB/3GB per day or 'unlimited')
throttlePolicy.notes: Trip.com sells both per-day allowances and fixed total-data packages (1GB–100GB). For the tiers marketed as unlimited, no daily high-speed cap or post-cap speed is published before purchase. Plan length is fully flexible from 1 to 365 days.

appAccess.notes: Vendor claim + user reports; not yet verified on the ground by us
Vendor claim + user reports; unverified by us
Multiple user reports; unverified by us
Multiple user reports; unverified by us
Marketed as 'ChatGPT available', but at least one buyer reports ChatGPT still required a VPN while other apps worked. Flagged as the key thing to verify in our field test.

pros: Runs on the three official Chinese carriers (China Mobile / Unicom / Telecom) — a fully legal, non-grey-market product with near-universal domestic coverage
Most flexible plan ladder of any provider here: 1 to 365 days, 0.5GB/day up to unlimited, plus fixed packages from 1GB to 100GB
Fully refundable at any time before the eSIM is installed — rare in this category
In-app installation without QR-code scanning; no Chinese ID or local number required
24/7 English-language support from a large OTA, plus the buyer protection that comes with booking through a major travel platform
cons: No published fair-use policy: neither the daily high-speed cap nor the throttled speed is disclosed before you buy
Firewall behaviour needs verification — it is sold on domestic carriers with international routing, and at least one buyer reports ChatGPT still being blocked
Small short-trip plans are not cheap per GB ($2.94 for 500MB works out to ~$5.88/GB)
eSIM is a side product for a travel platform, not a specialist network operator — less transparency about the underlying routing than dedicated eSIM brands
